does: This paper develops a Shapley value-based framework to quantify how individual adjectives steer the performance of large language models on the MMLU benchmark. The authors systematically analyze 100 adjectives across five diverse model architectures—o3, gpt-4o-mini, phi-3, llama-3-70b, and deepseek-r1—to move beyond informal prompting heuristics toward principled attribution of linguistic effects. The core finding is that a small set of adjectives exerts disproportionately large influence on model outputs, yet this influence varies significantly across models. The analysis reveals a "family effect" where models sharing architectural lineage show correlated sensitivity patterns, while architecturally distant models exhibit largely uncorrelated responses, demonstrating that effective prompting strategies cannot be universally applied across all LLMs.
connects: This work extends the emerging literature on prompt engineering and linguistic control of LLMs by introducing rigorous causal attribution methods previously applied in interpretability research. It builds on prior work exploring how natural language variations affect model behavior, but replaces ad-hoc observations with Shapley value decomposition—a principled approach from explainable AI that has been used to understand feature importance in neural networks. The paper responds to the fragmentation in prompting research, where findings from one model often fail to generalize, and connects to broader questions in mechanistic interpretability about how models process linguistic signals. It also engages with alignment-relevant concerns about whether we can reliably control model behavior through language.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing model-specific prompting strategies grounded in systematic understanding rather than trial-and-error. The discovery of family effects motivates future work to characterize sensitivity profiles for model lineages, potentially enabling faster adaptation when new models are released. The findings that adjective steering direction depends on syntactic or contextual factors point toward investigating how compositional linguistic structures interact with model internals, which could inform both better prompting techniques and improved training procedures for alignment. This work also suggests that measuring linguistic control through Shapley attribution could become a standard tool for evaluating and comparing how different architectures respond to natural language conditioning, supporting more reliable deployment of LLMs in domains requiring precise behavioral control.
